original_abstract:
When I was asked to reprint my article, “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” my first instinct was to refuse. I wrote the piece at twenty-three for a graduate seminar at UCLA, and like many early forays into research, it relied on a limited selection of primary sources. At the time, I had set my sights on a daunting and ultimately impractical dissertation comparing the Black Left in South Africa and the U.S. South—an endeavor made impossible by the fact that I couldn't actually enter South Africa. In 1985, President P. W. Botha had declared a state of emergency, and my active role in protests at the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills essentially guaranteed my visa would be denied. 

Revisiting the essay three decades later, parts of it are undeniably cringeworthy, particularly the rigid Marxist jargon and the archival gaps. In 1986, after all, the Soviet Union had not yet collapsed, and its archives remained firmly shut. Yet, despite the limitations of my youth and the available resources, I still find sections of the work I am proud of. Drawing on Cedric Robinson’s then-recent *Black Marxism*, I argued that a Black radical tradition—built on African redemption, rural resistance to the 1913 Land Act, and burgeoning nationalism—had already established a vision for self-determination long before the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA) arrived on the scene. 

In those early years, the CPSA was focused almost entirely on the white working class—famously backing strikes under the baffling slogan, “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” It was only when African trade unionists and Comintern officials in Moscow challenged this myopia, specifically criticizing the Party’s obsession with the 1922 Rand Revolt over the struggles of African miners, that the direction began to shift. Looking back, the essay's core contribution was its attempt to flip the standard narrative: it wasn't just that Communists "infiltrated" the nationalist movement, but rather that African nationalists fundamentally transformed the Communist Party, forcing a roadmap for Black majority rule and the return of stolen land.
humanized_abstract:
So when I was contacted by a magazine that wanted to re-print my article “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” a 10-page piece I wrote at 23 on the eve of entering graduate school at UCLA, my first reaction was “no way.” The piece is heavily based on selected secondary literature from the time. (I had had my sights set on an equally quixotic – and impossibly constructed – dissertation focused on a comparative history of the Black Left in the U.S. South and in South Africa, though I never expected to actually be able to visit South Africa to conduct the primary fieldwork. It’s hard to imagine how I would have managed to get a visa by this time, in any case, given my active role in demonstrations at the South African consulate in Beverly Hills).

Reading a 1986 essay of my youth has been quite a nostalgia trip over the past few weeks — scary embarrassing even — and I decided to revisit the parts that could be traced back to the original material; especially with all the cringe language and lack of materials in between. As of 1986 the Soviet block was still standing with its precious archives firmly locked and I was heavily influenced by whatever propaganda my time in ‘school’ taught me. In between that amount of nonsense and the more obviously leftist text I struggled to keep the momentum and interest in a ‘development’ which at the time seemed vastly “radical”, but in hindsight does have some worth; it’s one of my early pieces with even the early works of Cedric Robinson – Black Marxism - deeply influencing my early views of an independence liberation “schema” of African Redemption and the early forms of Land Resistance to the 1913 "greater Britain’s in South Africa than the Transvaal is capable of affording" to the the struggle and beginnings of the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA) long after any true ‘black radical' attempt at African Liberation and Redemptive politics as I see it had been conceived — much before even what I would define as a nationalist uprising, taking root and manifesting into an unfolding quest of independent liberation for the majority black people of the subcontinent.

You are not logged in. Log in to share this post! Bookmark and Share Apartheid in Colour It seems that in the early years of the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA), the Party concentrated almost exclusively on organizing the white working class, a concentration reflected in their slogan when supporting strikes and walksouts: “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” In Moscow, Comintern officials, and African trade unionists were complaining to the Party about the sole emphasis on the Rand Revolt of 1922 and the relative neglect of the very significant struggles taking place among black miners. The essay here attempts to tell the story in reverse: Rather than the communists “infiltrating” the nationalist movement of the time, it was the African leaders and through them the Comintern, who were responsible for bringing about a sea change in the make-up and intent of the Communist Party, one which in the end prepared the way for Black majority rule, and indeed, the return of the land to its rightful African owners.
